---
title: "Life Expectancy and Population"
---

## Introduction

This graph examines the relationship between population size and life expectancy across countries in 2007. Each point represents a country, and colors indicate continents. Population is shown on a logarithmic scale to account for large differences between countries.

```{python}
#| echo: true
import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

df = pd.read_csv("gapminder.csv")

# Use latest year to avoid repeated country points across time
latest_year = df["year"].max()
latest = df[df["year"] == latest_year].copy()

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
for cont, sub in latest.groupby("continent"):
    ax.scatter(sub["population_millions"], sub["life_expectancy"], label=cont, alpha=0.7)

ax.set_xscale("log")
ax.set_xlabel("Population (millions, log scale)")
ax.set_ylabel("Life expectancy")
ax.set_title(f"Life Expectancy vs Population by Continent ({latest_year})")
ax.legend()
plt.show()
```

## Interpretation

Unlike GDP per capita, population size does not show a strong relationship with life expectancy. Both small and large countries can have high or low life expectancy. This suggests that population size alone is not a key determinant of health outcomes. Instead, economic development and other institutional factors likely play a more important role.
